headline: Hormuz Flashpoint Dominates Pre-Market; Tech Tailwinds from Monday Carry Into Tuesday Open
body_html:
Tuesday's pre-market session opens under the shadow of fresh geopolitical turbulence in the Persian Gulf, where attacks on a Qatari liquefied-natural-gas tanker and a Saudi-flagged crude carrier near the Strait of Hormuz sent oil prices higher overnight. NATO allies have moved swiftly to convene discussions with Gulf Arab partners, and the situation remains fluid heading into the New York open. Traders will be watching crude benchmarks closely for any further escalation that could ripple through energy equities and broader risk sentiment.

Monday's completed session offered a more encouraging underpinning. Semiconductors led all sectors, gaining 2.39%, with connectivity-chip specialist Credo Technology surging 9.77% and networking giant Arista Networks adding 8.31%. Storage names also shone — Western Digital climbed 7.14% — pointing to sustained infrastructure-spending appetite. Breadth was modestly positive at 211 advancers versus 184 decliners across the universe of 396 names, suggesting the rally was selective rather than euphoric.

On the downside, Materials and Homebuilders were the session's weakest sectors, off 1.06% and 1.26% respectively, while auto-parts retailers O'Reilly Automotive and AutoZone fell more than 6.6% each. Key catalysts to monitor at the open: any fresh Hormuz headlines, Shell's upwardly revised second-quarter gas-output guidance, and whether Monday's semiconductor momentum extends into a second consecutive session.

briefs.headline: Strait of Hormuz Attacks Rattle Energy Markets Ahead of Open
briefs.body: Two tanker incidents near the Strait of Hormuz — one involving a Qatari LNG vessel and another a Saudi-flagged crude carrier — pushed oil prices higher overnight. The waterway handles a significant share of global energy transit, and any prolonged disruption would intensify supply concerns already simmering after Saudi Arabia trimmed its official crude selling prices.

briefs.headline: Saudi Price Cut Finds Cool Reception Among Asian Buyers
briefs.body: Riyadh's latest reduction in crude export prices has failed to stir meaningful demand among Asian refiners, according to traders, who say regional storage remains adequately supplied. The move raises questions about OPEC+ pricing power at a moment when Hormuz tensions are simultaneously threatening to tighten physical availability.

briefs.headline: Shell Lifts Gas-Output Outlook, Signals Stronger Trading Quarter
briefs.body: Shell raised its second-quarter natural-gas production guidance and flagged improved gas-trading results, offering a bright spot for energy investors navigating a volatile commodity backdrop. The update could lend relative support to integrated energy names at the open even as geopolitical risk weighs on the broader sector.

briefs.headline: NATO Gathers in Ankara Ahead of Trump Summit, Arms Deals on Display
briefs.body: Alliance defence ministers convened in Ankara ahead of a summit with President Trump, with significant weapons contracts showcased on the sidelines. Separately, the White House is reported to be preparing to offer Turkey a path back to the F-35 programme, a diplomatic shift that could have implications for regional security alignments and defence-industry order books.

briefs.headline: India Finalises Missile Supply Agreement with Indonesia
briefs.body: New Delhi is set to deliver BrahMos supersonic cruise missiles and Astra air-to-air missiles to Jakarta under a new bilateral defence agreement, according to an Indian government official. The deal underscores India's expanding role as an arms exporter and deepens strategic ties between the two Indo-Pacific nations.

briefs.headline: Monday's Semiconductor Surge Sets the Tone for Tuesday Watchers
briefs.body: Credo Technology's 9.77% advance, Arista Networks' 8.31% gain, and Western Digital's 7.14% rise combined to push the Semiconductors and Networking complex sharply higher on Monday, the session's standout theme. Investors will be watching whether pre-market futures sustain that momentum or whether Hormuz-driven risk aversion tempers the early bid in growth-tech names.
